revision.comment: Created by translating the page "[[:ja:Special:Redirect/revision/85995053|神社神道]]"
revision.text:
'''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of [[Shinto]]. It is used in the following two senses
* A pre-World War II variant of "[[State Shinto]]". * A form of Shinto centered on [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy and reverends.<ref>[[景山春樹]] 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref>
現在では単に「神道」という場合、神社神道を指す。祭祀の場となる神社は日本各地に数多くあるが、1945年までは全ての神社神道に属する神社が[[Home Ministry|内務省]]の外局である{{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}の管轄下におかれていた。1945年12月に[[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]]によって発せられた[[Shinto Directive|神道指令]]により、「神道の国家管理」は廃止されることになり、{{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}は廃止されて1[[Religious corporation|宗教法人]]として改組され、新たに[[Association of Shinto Shrines|神社本庁]]が発足した。{{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}の管轄下にあった神社神道約8万社は、
Today, when the term "Shinto" is used simply, it refers to Shrine Shinto. Shinto shrines are numerous throughout Japan, but until 1945, all Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}, an external bureau of the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of Home Affairs]]. {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as a single religious corporation, and the new [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] was established. 80,000 Shinto shrines under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} were
# Those that came under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Main Office of Shinto Shrines]] when it was established (approx. 7,800 shrines) # Those that came under the umbrella of a different umbrella organization from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|main shrine headquarters]]. # Ones that became {{ill|Standalone Shrine|lt=Standalone Shrines|ja|単立}}
神社神道には教典は存在せず、『[[Kojiki|古事記]]』や『[[Nihon Shoki|日本書紀]]』などの神典にのっとり祭祀をおこなう。 祭祀の担い手となるのは[[Kannushi|神職]]であり、宮司・禰宜・[[Kannushi|権禰宜]]・出仕などの役職につき、神事を司る。 「神社神道の[[Miko|巫女]]」は神職には含まれない。「[[Kagura|神楽]]の舞手」として「神事に参加」することはできるが、「神事を主宰・執行」することはできない。
神職となるためには「[[Japanese court rank|位階]]」を必要とする。 位階は、現在では[[Association of Shinto Shrines|神社本庁]]が授与する'''民間の資格'''で、浄階・明階・正階・権正階・直階の５段階がある。
-There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == かつて神道は[[Shinto Secretariat|神道事務局]]によって管理されようとしたが、最終的に神道事務局は[[Shinto Taikyo|神道大教]]とする[[Sect Shinto|教派神道]]ととして神社神道とは切り離された。その後神社神道は[[Home Ministry|内務省]]によって管理されたが、[[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|国民精神総動員運動]]の影響を受け[[1940|1940年]]（[[1940|昭和15年]]）には神祇院として独立した。しかし直後の敗戦によって目立った成果は上げなかった。
戦後は神祇院の後継組織である[[Association of Shinto Shrines|神社本庁]]が最も多くの神社が所属する団体であるが、神社本庁は原則として[[Religious corporation|宗教法人]]格を有することが加盟の条件であるため法人格を有しない小規模な祠等はそもそも所属できない。また宗教法人格を有する神社に限っても、例えば[[Higashiōsaka|東大阪市]]は法人格を持つ神社の半数以上が神社本庁未加盟でありその中には[[Engishiki Jinmyocho|式内社]]の石切剣箭神社等も含まれている<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>。さらに全国でも[[Kamakura-gū|鎌倉宮]]・[[Yasukuni Shrine|靖国神社]]・[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|伏見稲荷大社]]・[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|日光東照宮]]・[[Keta Shrine|気多大社]]・梨木神社・新熊野神社・[[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|富岡八幡宮]]・[[Hinokuma Shrine|日前宮]]など、有名な神社であるにもかかわらず神社本庁の包括下にない例も存在する。
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "shrine Shinto." == 神社神道という言葉は比較的新しく、[[Meiji (era)|明治]]以降、[[Sect Shinto|教派神道]]と区別するためにつくられた。1868年（明治元年）、[[Meiji Restoration|維新政府]]は神祇官（明治時代）を設置したが、中央集権的体制を強めるためには[[State Shinto|国家神道]]政策だけでは足りないとして[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇省]]を設置し皇道宣布運動を展開する<ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>。
ただ、[[1882|1882年]]（明治15年）1月24日には内務省達乙第7号「自今神官ハ教導職二兼補ヲ廃シ葬儀二関係セサルモノトス」により、「'''神社神道は宗教ではない'''<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>」（[[Secular Shrine Theory|神社非宗教論]]）とし、[[Religion|宗教]]（教派神道・仏教）と[[Festival|祭祀]]（神社神道）を分離させ、[[Shinto|神道]]は国の祭祀として非宗教とされた。
1899年（明治32年）の宗教法案には[[Shinto|神道]]や[[Christianity|キリスト教]]は含まれていなかったが、明治末頃から、教派神道は国家神道と称されはじめた。
1917年（大正6年）になると、日本基督教会が、学生が神社参拝を強制されていることを理由に[[Secular Shrine Theory|神社非宗教論]]を否定した（『神社に関する決議』)。さらに強制は[[Meiji Constitution|憲法]]の[[Freedom of religion|信教の自由]]に抵触するものであるという不服が申立てられた<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>。
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "kokutai shinto" and "shrine shinto. <ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref>。Before World War II, the term shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.

'''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of [[Shinto]]. It is used in the following two senses
* A pre-World War II variant of "[[State Shinto]]". * A form of Shinto centered on [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy and reverends.<ref>[[景山春樹]] 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref>

Today, when the term "Shinto" is used simply, it refers to Shrine Shinto. Shinto shrines are numerous throughout Japan, but until 1945, all Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}, an external bureau of the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of Home Affairs]]. {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as a single religious corporation, and the new [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] was established. 80,000 Shinto shrines under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} were
# Those that came under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Main Office of Shinto Shrines]] when it was established (approx. 7,800 shrines) # Those that came under the umbrella of a different umbrella organization from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|main shrine headquarters]]. # Ones that became {{ill|Standalone Shrine|lt=Standalone Shrines|ja|単立}}
神社神道には教典は存在せず、『[[Kojiki|古事記]]』や『[[Nihon Shoki|日本書紀]]』などの神典にのっとり祭祀をおこなう。 祭祀の担い手となるのは[[Kannushi|神職]]であり、宮司・禰宜・[[Kannushi|権禰宜]]・出仕などの役職につき、神事を司る。 「神社神道の[[Miko|巫女]]」は神職には含まれない。「[[Kagura|神楽]]の舞手」として「神事に参加」することはできるが、「神事を主宰・執行」することはできない。
神職となるためには「[[Japanese court rank|位階]]」を必要とする。 位階は、現在では[[Association of Shinto Shrines|神社本庁]]が授与する'''民間の資格'''で、浄階・明階・正階・権正階・直階の５段階がある。
-There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat|Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]], but under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940|1940]] ([[1940|Showa 15 1940|1940]]), it became independent as a Shinto shrine. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
戦後は神祇院の後継組織である[[Association of Shinto Shrines|神社本庁]]が最も多くの神社が所属する団体であるが、神社本庁は原則として[[Religious corporation|宗教法人]]格を有することが加盟の条件であるため法人格を有しない小規模な祠等はそもそも所属できない。また宗教法人格を有する神社に限っても、例えば[[Higashiōsaka|東大阪市]]は法人格を持つ神社の半数以上が神社本庁未加盟でありその中には[[Engishiki Jinmyocho|式内社]]の石切剣箭神社等も含まれている<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>。さらに全国でも[[Kamakura-gū|鎌倉宮]]・[[Yasukuni Shrine|靖国神社]]・[[Fushimi Inari-taisha|伏見稲荷大社]]・[[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|日光東照宮]]・[[Keta Shrine|気多大社]]・梨木神社・新熊野神社・[[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|富岡八幡宮]]・[[Hinokuma Shrine|日前宮]]など、有名な神社であるにもかかわらず神社本庁の包括下にない例も存在する。
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>

There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat|Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]], but under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940|1940]] ([[1940|Showa 15 1940|1940]]), it became independent as a Shinto shrine. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>. In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Tōshō-gū]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Nashiki-jinja]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Nashiki Shrine Nashiki Shrine]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "shrine Shinto." == 神社神道という言葉は比較的新しく、[[Meiji (era)|明治]]以降、[[Sect Shinto|教派神道]]と区別するためにつくられた。1868年（明治元年）、[[Meiji Restoration|維新政府]]は神祇官（明治時代）を設置したが、中央集権的体制を強めるためには[[State Shinto|国家神道]]政策だけでは足りないとして[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇省]]を設置し皇道宣布運動を展開する<ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>。
ただ、[[1882|1882年]]（明治15年）1月24日には内務省達乙第7号「自今神官ハ教導職二兼補ヲ廃シ葬儀二関係セサルモノトス」により、「'''神社神道は宗教ではない'''<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>」（[[Secular Shrine Theory|神社非宗教論]]）とし、[[Religion|宗教]]（教派神道・仏教）と[[Festival|祭祀]]（神社神道）を分離させ、[[Shinto|神道]]は国の祭祀として非宗教とされた。
1899年（明治32年）の宗教法案には[[Shinto|神道]]や[[Christianity|キリスト教]]は含まれていなかったが、明治末頃から、教派神道は国家神道と称されはじめた。
1917年（大正6年）になると、日本基督教会が、学生が神社参拝を強制されていることを理由に[[Secular Shrine Theory|神社非宗教論]]を否定した（『神社に関する決議』)。さらに強制は[[Meiji Constitution|憲法]]の[[Freedom of religion|信教の自由]]に抵触するものであるという不服が申立てられた<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>。
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "kokutai shinto" and "shrine shinto. <ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref>。Before World War II, the term shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.

== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book |title=[[世界大百科事典]] |date=1978年印刷 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |volume=16巻 |access-date=2009-05-03 |orig-date=1972-04-25|和書}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

'''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of [[Shinto]]. It is used in the following two senses
* A pre-World War II variant of "[[State Shinto]]". * A form of Shinto centered on [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy and reverends.<ref>[[景山春樹]] 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref>
現在では単に「神道」という場合、神社神道を指す。祭祀の場となる神社は日本各地に数多くあるが、1945年までは全ての神社神道に属する神社が[[Home Ministry|内務省]]の外局である{{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}の管轄下におかれていた。1945年12月に[[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]]によって発せられた[[Shinto Directive|神道指令]]により、「神道の国家管理」は廃止されることになり、{{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}は廃止されて1[[Religious corporation|宗教法人]]として改組され、新たに[[Association of Shinto Shrines|神社本庁]]が発足した。{{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}の管轄下にあった神社神道約8万社は、
Today, when the term "Shinto" is used simply, it refers to Shrine Shinto. Shinto shrines are numerous throughout Japan, but until 1945, all Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}, an external bureau of the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of Home Affairs]]. {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as a single religious corporation, and the new [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] was established. 80,000 Shinto shrines under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} were
# Those that came under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Main Office of Shinto Shrines]] when it was established (approx. 7,800 shrines) # Those that came under the umbrella of a different umbrella organization from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|main shrine headquarters]]. # Ones that became {{ill|Standalone Shrine|lt=Standalone Shrines|ja|単立}}
神社神道には教典は存在せず、『[[Kojiki|古事記]]』や『[[Nihon Shoki|日本書紀]]』などの神典にのっとり祭祀をおこなう。 祭祀の担い手となるのは[[Kannushi|神職]]であり、宮司・禰宜・[[Kannushi|権禰宜]]・出仕などの役職につき、神事を司る。 「神社神道の[[Miko|巫女]]」は神職には含まれない。「[[Kagura|神楽]]の舞手」として「神事に参加」することはできるが、「神事を主宰・執行」することはできない。
神職となるためには「[[Japanese court rank|位階]]」を必要とする。 位階は、現在では[[Association of Shinto Shrines|神社本庁]]が授与する'''民間の資格'''で、浄階・明階・正階・権正階・直階の５段階がある。
-There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>

The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]], but under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940|1940]] ([[1940|Showa 15 1940|1940]]), it became independent as a Shinto shrine. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>. In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Tōshō-gū]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Nashiki-jinja]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Nashiki Shrine Nashiki Shrine]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "shrine Shinto." == 神社神道という言葉は比較的新しく、[[Meiji (era)|明治]]以降、[[Sect Shinto|教派神道]]と区別するためにつくられた。1868年（明治元年）、[[Meiji Restoration|維新政府]]は神祇官（明治時代）を設置したが、中央集権的体制を強めるためには[[State Shinto|国家神道]]政策だけでは足りないとして[[Ministry of Shinto|神祇省]]を設置し皇道宣布運動を展開する<ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>。
However, on January 24, [[1882|1882]] (Meiji 15), the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that '''[[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto|Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto|Shinto]] or [[Christianity|Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected the [[Secular Shrine Theory|Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were being forced to worship at shrines ("Resolution on Shrines"). A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[Freedom of religion|Freedom of religion]] of the [[Meiji Constitution|Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>。
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "kokutai shinto" and "shrine shinto. <ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref>。Before World War II, the term shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.

== Origin of the term "shrine Shinto." == The term shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. 1868 (Meiji 1), the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Shintoism Office (Meiji era)], but to strengthen the centralized However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched. <ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>。
However, on January 24, [[1882|1882]] (Meiji 15), the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that '''[[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto|Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>

The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "kokutai shinto" and "shrine shinto. <ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref>。Before World War II, the term shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book |title=[[世界大百科事典]] |date=1978年印刷 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |volume=16巻 |access-date=2009-05-03 |orig-date=1972-04-25|和書}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

'''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of [[Shinto]]. It is used in the following two senses
* A pre-World War II variant of "[[State Shinto]]". * A form of Shinto centered on [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy and reverends.<ref>[[景山春樹]] 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref>
現在では単に「神道」という場合、神社神道を指す。祭祀の場となる神社は日本各地に数多くあるが、1945年までは全ての神社神道に属する神社が[[Home Ministry|内務省]]の外局である{{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}の管轄下におかれていた。1945年12月に[[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]]によって発せられた[[Shinto Directive|神道指令]]により、「神道の国家管理」は廃止されることになり、{{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}は廃止されて1[[Religious corporation|宗教法人]]として改組され、新たに[[Association of Shinto Shrines|神社本庁]]が発足した。{{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}の管轄下にあった神社神道約8万社は、
Today, when the term "Shinto" is used simply, it refers to Shrine Shinto. Shinto shrines are numerous throughout Japan, but until 1945, all Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}, an external bureau of the [[Home Ministry|Ministry of Home Affairs]]. {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as a single religious corporation, and the new [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] was established. 80,000 Shinto shrines under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} were
# Those that came under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Main Office of Shinto Shrines]] when it was established (approx. 7,800 shrines) # Those that came under the umbrella of a different umbrella organization from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|main shrine headquarters]]. # Ones that became {{ill|Standalone Shrine|lt=Standalone Shrines|ja|単立}}
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. The [[Kannushi|Kannushi]] are in charge of the rituals, and hold such positions as priest, priest-in-charge, [[Kannushi|Kannushi|Gon-Negishi]], and priest-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[Miko|miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura|Kagura]] dancer may "participate in Shinto rituals," but may not "preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
In order to become a priest, one must have a "[[Japanese court rank|rank]]". The rank is a '''civilian qualification''' granted by the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], and there are five levels of rank: Jo-ki, Mei-ki, Sei-ki, Gon-sei-ki, and Nao-ki.
-There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat|Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]], but under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940|1940]] ([[1940|Showa 15 1940|1940]]), it became independent as a Shinto shrine. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>. In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Tōshō-gū]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Nashiki-jinja]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Nashiki Shrine Nashiki Shrine]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "shrine Shinto." == The term shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. 1868 (Meiji 1), the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Shintoism Office (Meiji era)], but to strengthen the centralized However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched. <ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>。
However, on January 24, [[1882|1882]] (Meiji 15), the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that '''[[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto|Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto|Shinto]] or [[Christianity|Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected the [[Secular Shrine Theory|Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were being forced to worship at shrines ("Resolution on Shrines"). A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[Freedom of religion|Freedom of religion]] of the [[Meiji Constitution|Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>。
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "kokutai shinto" and "shrine shinto. <ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref>。Before World War II, the term shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.

In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>. In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Tōshō-gū]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Nashiki-jinja]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Nashiki Shrine Nashiki Shrine]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "shrine Shinto." == The term shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. 1868 (Meiji 1), the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Shintoism Office (Meiji era)], but to strengthen the centralized However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched. <ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>。
However, on January 24, [[1882|1882]] (Meiji 15), the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that '''[[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto|Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto|Shinto]] or [[Christianity|Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected the [[Secular Shrine Theory|Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were being forced to worship at shrines ("Resolution on Shrines"). A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[Freedom of religion|Freedom of religion]] of the [[Meiji Constitution|Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>。
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "kokutai shinto" and "shrine shinto. <ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref>。Before World War II, the term shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
== References == {{脚注ヘルプ}} <references />
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book |title=[[世界大百科事典]] |date=1978年印刷 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |volume=16巻 |access-date=2009-05-03 |orig-date=1972-04-25|和書}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

'''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of [[Shinto]]. It is used in the following two senses
* A pre-World War II variant of "[[State Shinto]]". * A form of Shinto centered on [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy and reverends.<ref>[[景山春樹]] 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref>
Today, when the term "Shinto" is used simply, it refers to Shrines Shinto. There are many shrines throughout Japan that serve as places of worship, but until 1945, all shrines belonging to Shinto were under the jurisdiction of the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}, an external department of the [[Home Ministry|Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945, the "State Administration of Shinto" was abolished and the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as one [[Religious corporation|Religious corporation]], and a new [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Honcho]] was established. The {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as one [[Religious corporation|Religious corporation]], and a new [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Honcho]] was established.
80,000 Shinto shrines under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} were
# Those that came under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Main Office of Shinto Shrines]] when it was established (approx. 7,800 shrines) # Those that came under the umbrella of a different umbrella organization from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|main shrine headquarters]]. # Ones that became {{ill|Standalone Shrine|lt=Standalone Shrines|ja|単立}}
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. The [[Kannushi|Kannushi]] are in charge of the rituals, and hold such positions as priest, priest-in-charge, [[Kannushi|Kannushi|Gon-Negishi]], and priest-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[Miko|miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura|Kagura]] dancer may "participate in Shinto rituals," but may not "preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat|Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]], but under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940|1940]] ([[1940|Showa 15 1940|1940]]), it became independent as a Shinto shrine. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>. In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Tōshō-gū]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Nashiki-jinja]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Nashiki Shrine Nashiki Shrine]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "shrine Shinto." == The term shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. 1868 (Meiji 1), the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Shintoism Office (Meiji era)], but to strengthen the centralized However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched. <ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>。
However, on January 24, [[1882|1882]] (Meiji 15), the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that '''[[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto|Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>

In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected the [[Secular Shrine Theory|Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were being forced to worship at shrines ("Resolution on Shrines"). A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[Freedom of religion|Freedom of religion]] of the [[Meiji Constitution|Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>。
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "kokutai shinto" and "shrine shinto. <ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref>。Before World War II, the term shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.

After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>. In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Tōshō-gū]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Nashiki-jinja]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Nashiki Shrine Nashiki Shrine]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "shrine Shinto." == The term shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. 1868 (Meiji 1), the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Shintoism Office (Meiji era)], but to strengthen the centralized However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched. <ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>。
However, on January 24, [[1882|1882]] (Meiji 15), the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto|Shinto]] or [[Christianity|Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected the [[Secular Shrine Theory|Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were being forced to worship at shrines ("Resolution on Shrines"). A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[Freedom of religion|Freedom of religion]] of the [[Meiji Constitution|Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>。
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "kokutai shinto" and "shrine shinto. <ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref>。Before World War II, the term shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
== References ==
<references />
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book |title=[[世界大百科事典]] |date=1978年印刷 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |volume=16巻 |access-date=2009-05-03 |orig-date=1972-04-25|和書}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

'''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of [[Shinto]]. It is used in the following two senses
* A pre-World War II variant of "[[State Shinto]]". * A form of Shinto centered on [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy and reverends.<ref>[[景山春樹]] 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref>
Today, when the term "Shinto" is used simply, it refers to Shrines Shinto. There are many shrines throughout Japan that serve as places of worship, but until 1945, all shrines belonging to Shinto were under the jurisdiction of the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}, an external department of the [[Home Ministry|Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945, the "State Administration of Shinto" was abolished and the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as one [[Religious corporation|Religious corporation]], and a new [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Honcho]] was established. The {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as one [[Religious corporation|Religious corporation]], and a new [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Honcho]] was established.
80,000 Shinto shrines under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} were
# Those that came under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Main Office of Shinto Shrines]] when it was established (approx. 7,800 shrines) # Those that came under the umbrella of a different umbrella organization from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|main shrine headquarters]]. # Ones that became {{ill|Standalone Shrine|lt=Standalone Shrines|ja|単立}}
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. The [[Kannushi|Kannushi]] are in charge of the rituals, and hold such positions as priest, priest-in-charge, [[Kannushi|Kannushi|Gon-Negishi]], and priest-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[Miko|miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura|Kagura]] dancer may "participate in Shinto rituals," but may not "preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat|Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]], but under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940|1940]] ([[1940|Showa 15 1940|1940]]), it became independent as a Shinto shrine. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>. In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Tōshō-gū]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Nashiki-jinja]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Nashiki Shrine Nashiki Shrine]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "shrine Shinto." == The term shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. 1868 (Meiji 1), the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]], but to strengthen the centralized However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched. <ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>。
However, on January 24, [[1882|1882]] (Meiji 15), the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>

In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected the [[Secular Shrine Theory|Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were being forced to worship at shrines ("Resolution on Shrines"). A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[Freedom of religion|Freedom of religion]] of the [[Meiji Constitution|Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>。
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "kokutai shinto" and "shrine shinto. <ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref>。Before World War II, the term shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.

The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]], but under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940|1940]] ([[1940|Showa 15 1940|1940]]), it became independent as a Shinto shrine. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>. In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Tōshō-gū]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Nashiki-jinja]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Nashiki Shrine Nashiki Shrine]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "Shrine Shinto." == The term Shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. 1868 (Meiji 1), the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]], but to strengthen the centralized However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched. <ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>。
However, on January 24, [[1882|1882]] (Meiji 15), the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (Shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto|Shinto]] or [[Christianity|Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected the [[Secular Shrine Theory|Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were being forced to worship at shrines ("Resolution on Shrines"). A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[Freedom of religion|Freedom of religion]] of the [[Meiji Constitution|Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>。
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "kokutai shinto" and "Shrine Shinto. <ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref>。Before World War II, the term Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
== References ==
<references />
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book |title=[[世界大百科事典]] |date=1978年印刷 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |volume=16巻 |access-date=2009-05-03 |orig-date=1972-04-25|和書}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

{{Short description|Mainstream non-doctrinal Shinto}}
'''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of [[Shinto]]. It is used in the following two senses
* A pre-World War II variant of "[[State Shinto]]". * A form of Shinto centered on [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy and reverends.<ref>[[景山春樹]] 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref>
Today, when the term "Shinto" is used simply, it refers to Shrines Shinto. There are many shrines throughout Japan that serve as places of worship, but until 1945, all shrines belonging to Shinto were under the jurisdiction of the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}, an external department of the [[Home Ministry|Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945, the "State Administration of Shinto" was abolished and the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as one [[Religious corporation|Religious corporation]], and a new [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Honcho]] was established. The {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as one [[Religious corporation|Religious corporation]], and a new [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Honcho]] was established.
80,000 Shinto shrines under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} were
# Those that came under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Main Office of Shinto Shrines]] when it was established (approx. 7,800 shrines) # Those that came under the umbrella of a different umbrella organization from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|main shrine headquarters]]. # Ones that became {{ill|Standalone Shrine|lt=Standalone Shrines|ja|単立}}
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as [[Kojiki|Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki|Nihon Shoki]]. The [[Kannushi|Kannushi]] are in charge of the rituals, and hold such positions as priest, priest-in-charge, [[Kannushi|Kannushi|Gon-Negishi]], and priest-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[Miko|miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura|Kagura]] dancer may "participate in Shinto rituals," but may not "preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat|Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]], but under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940|1940]], it became independent as a Shinto shrine. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>. In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Tōshō-gū]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Nashiki-jinja]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Nashiki Shrine Nashiki Shrine]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine|Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>

However, on January 24, [[1882|1882]] (Meiji 15), the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (Shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>

There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]], but under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940]], it became independent as a Shinto shrine. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref> In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Tōshō-gū]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Nashiki-jinja]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Nashiki Shrine Nashiki Shrine]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "Shrine Shinto." == The term Shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. 1868 (Meiji 1), the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]], but to strengthen the centralized However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched.<ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
However, on January 24, [[1882]] (Meiji 15), the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (Shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected the [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were being forced to worship at shrines ("Resolution on Shrines"). A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[Freedom of religion]] of the [[Meiji Constitution|Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "kokutai shinto" and "Shrine Shinto.<ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, the term Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
