<references />
== Bibliography ==
* {{Cite book |title=[[世界大百科事典]] |date=1978年印刷 |publisher=[[平凡社]] |volume=16巻 |access-date=2009-05-03 |orig-date=1972-04-25|和書}} [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]]

{{Short description|Mainstream non-doctrinal Shinto}}
'''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of [[Shinto]]. It is used in the following two senses
* A pre-World War II variant of "[[State Shinto]]". * A form of Shinto centered on [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy and reverends.<ref>[[景山春樹]] 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref>
Today, when the term "Shinto" is used simply, it refers to Shrines Shinto. There are many shrines throughout Japan that serve as places of worship, but until 1945, all shrines belonging to Shinto were under the jurisdiction of the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}, an external department of the [[Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945, the "State Administration of Shinto" was abolished and the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as one [[Religious corporation]], and a new [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Honcho]] was established. The {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as one [[Religious corporation]], and a new [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Honcho]] was established.
80,000 Shinto shrines under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} were
# Those that came under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Main Office of Shinto Shrines]] when it was established (approx. 7,800 shrines) # Those that came under the umbrella of a different umbrella organization from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|main shrine headquarters]]. # Ones that became {{ill|Standalone Shrine|lt=Standalone Shrines|ja|単立}}
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]]. The [[Kannushi]] are in charge of the rituals, and hold such positions as priest, priest-in-charge, [[Kannushi|Kannushi|Gon-Negishi]], and priest-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura]] dancer may "participate in Shinto rituals," but may not "preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]], but under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940]], it became independent as a Shinto shrine. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref> In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Tōshō-gū]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Nashiki-jinja]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Nashiki Shrine Nashiki Shrine]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "Shrine Shinto." == The term Shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. 1868 (Meiji 1), the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]], but to strengthen the centralized However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched.<ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
However, on January 24, [[1882]] (Meiji 15), the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (Shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>

The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura]] dancer may "participate in Shinto rituals," but may not "preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]], but under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940]], it became independent as a Shinto shrine. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref> In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], {{ill|Nikko Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}, [[Keta Shrine]], [[Nashiki-jinja]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Nashiki Shrine Nashiki Shrine]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "Shrine Shinto." == The term Shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. 1868 (Meiji 1), the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]], but to strengthen the centralized However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched.<ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
However, on January 24, [[1882]] (Meiji 15), the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (Shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected the [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were being forced to worship at shrines ("Resolution on Shrines"). A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[Freedom of religion]] of the [[Meiji Constitution|Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "kokutai shinto" and "Shrine Shinto.<ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, the term Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.

{{copy edit|date=May 2022}}
{{Short description|Mainstream non-doctrinal Shinto}}
'''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of [[Shinto]]. It is used in the following two senses
* A pre-World War II variant of "[[State Shinto]]". * A form of Shinto centered on [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy and reverends.<ref>[[景山春樹]] 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref>
Today, when the term "Shinto" is used simply, it refers to Shrines Shinto. There are many shrines throughout Japan that serve as places of worship, but until 1945, all shrines belonging to Shinto were under the jurisdiction of the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}, an external department of the [[Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945, the "State Administration of Shinto" was abolished and the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as one [[Religious corporation]], and a new [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Honcho]] was established. The {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as one [[Religious corporation]], and a new [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Shinto Honcho]] was established.
80,000 Shinto shrines under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} were
# Those that came under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Main Office of Shinto Shrines]] when it was established (approx. 7,800 shrines) # Those that came under the umbrella of a different umbrella organization from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|main shrine headquarters]]. # Ones that became {{ill|Standalone Shrine|lt=Standalone Shrines|ja|単立}}
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]]. The [[Kannushi]] are in charge of the rituals, and hold such positions as priest, priest-in-charge, [[Kannushi|Kannushi|Gon-Negishi]], and priest-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura]] dancer may "participate in Shinto rituals," but may not "preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>

The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]], but under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940]], it became independent as a Shinto shrine. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref> In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], {{ill|Nikko Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}, [[Keta Shrine]], {{ill|Nashiki-jinja|ja|梨木神社}}, [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Nashiki Shrine Nashiki Shrine]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>

The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura]] dancer may "participate in Shinto rituals," but may not "preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]], but under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940]], it became independent as a Shinto shrine. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref> In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], {{ill|Nikko Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}, [[Keta Shrine]], {{ill|Nashiki-jinja|ja|梨木神社}}, [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "Shrine Shinto." == The term Shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. 1868 (Meiji 1), the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]], but to strengthen the centralized However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched.<ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
However, on January 24, [[1882]] (Meiji 15), the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (Shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected the [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were being forced to worship at shrines ("Resolution on Shrines"). A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[Freedom of religion]] of the [[Meiji Constitution|Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "kokutai shinto" and "Shrine Shinto.<ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, the term Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.

{{copy edit|date=May 2022}}
{{Short description|Mainstream non-doctrinal Shinto}}
'''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of [[Shinto]]. It is used in the following two senses
* A pre-World War II variant of "[[State Shinto]]". * A form of Shinto centered on [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy and reverends.<ref>[[景山春樹]] 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref>
Today, when the term "Shinto" is used simply, it refers to Shrines Shinto. There are many shrines throughout Japan that serve as places of worship, but until 1945, all shrines belonging to Shinto were under the jurisdiction of the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}, an external department of the [[Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945, the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as one [[Religious corporation]], and a new [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] was established.
80,000 Shinto shrines under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} were
# Those that came under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Main Office of Shinto Shrines]] when it was established (approx. 7,800 shrines) # Those that came under the umbrella of a different umbrella organization from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. # Ones that became {{ill|Standalone Shrine|lt=Standalone Shrines|ja|単立}}
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]]. The [[Kannushi]] are in charge of the rituals, and hold such positions as priest, priest-in-charge, [[Kannushi]], and priest-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura]] dancer may "participate in Shinto rituals," but may not "preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for the priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>

However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref> In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], {{ill|Nikko Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}, [[Keta Shrine]], {{ill|Nashiki-jinja|ja|梨木神社}}, [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.

The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura]] dancer may "participate in Shinto rituals," but may not "preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for the priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]], but under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940]], it became independent as a Shinto shrine. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref> In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], {{ill|Nikko Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}, [[Keta Shrine]], {{ill|Nashiki-jinja|ja|梨木神社}}, [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "Shrine Shinto." == The term Shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]], but to strengthen the centralized However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched.<ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
However, on January 24, [[1882]], the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (Shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected the [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were being forced to worship at shrines ("Resolution on Shrines"). A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[Freedom of religion]] of the [[Meiji Constitution|Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "[[State Shinto]]" and "Shrine Shinto.<ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, the term Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
== See also == *[[Seicho-no-Ie]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Secular Shrine Theory]] *[[Shinto sects and schools]] *[[Sect Shinto]] [[Category:Shinto]]

{{copy edit|date=May 2022}}
{{Short description|Mainstream non-doctrinal Shinto}}
'''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of [[Shinto]]. It is used in the following two senses
* A pre-World War II variant of "[[State Shinto]]". * A form of Shinto centered on [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy and reverends.<ref>[[景山春樹]] 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref>
Today, when the term "Shinto" is used simply, it refers to Shrine Shinto. There are many shrines throughout Japan that serve as places of worship, but until 1945, all shrines belonging to Shinto were under the jurisdiction of the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}, an external department of the [[Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945, the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} was abolished and reorganized as one [[Religious corporation]], and a new [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] was established.
80,000 Shinto shrines under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} were
# Those that came under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] when it was established (approx. 7,800 shrines) # Those that came under the umbrella of a different umbrella organization from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. # Ones that became {{ill|Standalone Shrine|lt=Standalone Shrines|ja|単立}}
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]]. The [[Kannushi]] are in charge of the rituals, and hold such positions as priest, priest-in-charge, [[Kannushi]], and priest-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura]] dancer may "participate in Shinto rituals," but may not "preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for the priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the umbrella of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]], but under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940]], it became independent as a Shinto shrine. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong, but since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref> In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], {{ill|Nikko Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}, [[Keta Shrine]], {{ill|Nashiki-jinja|ja|梨木神社}}, [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>

{{copy edit|date=May 2022}}
{{Short description|Mainstream non-doctrinal Shinto}}
'''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of [[Shinto]]. It is used in the following two senses:
* A pre-World War II variant of "[[State Shinto]]". * A form of Shinto centered on [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy and reverends.<ref>[[景山春樹]] 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref>
Today, when the term "Shinto" is used simply, it refers to Shrine Shinto. There are many shrines throughout Japan that serve as places of worship, but until 1945 all shrines belonging to Shinto were under the jurisdiction of the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}, an external department of the [[Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945 abolished the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} and was reorganized as one [[Religious corporation]], establishing a new [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
There are 80,000 Shinto shrines under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}:
# Those that came under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] when it was established (approx. 7,800 shrines). # Those that came under the umbrella of a different umbrella organization from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. # Ones that became {{ill|Standalone Shrine|lt=Standalone Shrines|ja|単立}}.
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]]. The [[Kannushi]] are in charge of the rituals, and they hold such positions as priest, priest-in-charge, [[Kannushi]], and administer of the rituals. The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura]] dancer may "participate in Shinto rituals," but may not "preside over or perform Shinto rituals."
There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for the priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the guidance of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in [[1940]], becoming independent. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong. But since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref> In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], {{ill|Nikko Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}, [[Keta Shrine]], {{ill|Nashiki-jinja|ja|梨木神社}}, [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>

In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to strengthen the centralized. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched.<ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, [[1882]], the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (Shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>

In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected the [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were being forced to worship at shrines ("Resolution on Shrines"). A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[Freedom of religion]] of the [[Meiji Constitution|Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>

Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "[[State Shinto]]" and "Shrine Shinto.<ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, the term Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.

The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura]] dancer may "participate in Shinto rituals," but may not "preside over or perform Shinto rituals."
There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for the priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the guidance of an umbrella organization other than the Jinja Honcho or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the Jinja Honcho" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong. But since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the Jinja Honcho, including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref> In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], {{ill|Nikko Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}, [[Keta Shrine]], {{ill|Nashiki-jinja|ja|梨木神社}}, [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the Jinja Honcho. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "Shrine Shinto." == The term Shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to strengthen the centralized. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched.<ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (Shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref> [[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected the [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were being forced to worship at shrines ("Resolution on Shrines"). A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[Freedom of religion]] of the [[Meiji Constitution|Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "[[State Shinto]]" and "Shrine Shinto.<ref> [[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, the term Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.

{{copy edit|date=May 2022}}
{{Short description|Mainstream non-doctrinal Shinto}}
'''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of [[Shinto]]. It is used in the following two senses:
* A pre-World War II variant of "[[State Shinto]]". * A form of Shinto centered on [[Shinto shrine|Shinto shrines]] after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy and reverends.<ref>[[景山春樹]] 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref>
Today, when the term "Shinto" is used simply, it refers to Shrine Shinto. There are many shrines throughout Japan that serve as places of worship, but until 1945 all shrines belonging to Shinto were under the jurisdiction of the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}, an external department of the [[Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945 abolished the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} and was reorganized as one [[Religious corporation]], establishing a new [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
There are 80,000 Shinto shrines under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}:
# Those that came under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] when it was established (approx. 7,800 shrines). # Those that came under the umbrella of a different umbrella organization from the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. # Ones that became {{ill|Standalone Shrine|lt=Standalone Shrines|ja|単立}}.
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]]. The [[Kannushi]] are in charge of the rituals, and they hold such positions as priest, priest-in-charge, [[Kannushi]], and administer of the rituals. The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura]] dancer may "participate in Shinto rituals," but may not "preside over or perform Shinto rituals."
There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for the priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the guidance of an umbrella organization other than the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref> 「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref> [https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong. But since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref> [http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref> In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], {{ill|Nikko Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}, [[Keta Shrine]], {{ill|Nashiki-jinja|ja|梨木神社}}, [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref> 『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "Shrine Shinto." == The term Shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to strengthen the centralized. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched.<ref> [[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>

The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura]] dancer may "participate in Shinto rituals," but may not "preside over or perform Shinto rituals."
There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for the priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the guidance of an umbrella organization other than the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks," and these educational institutions accept students from "shrines not under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]" and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref>「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref>[https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once attempted to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]] with the Shinto Secretariat as [[Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent. However, due to the defeat of the war soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], the successor to the Jingion, is the organization to which the largest number of shrines belong. But since the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership in principle, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible. Therefore, small shrines without juridical personality are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with religious juridical personality, for example, in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of the shrines with juridical personality are not members of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], including the [[Engishiki Jinmyocho|Shiki-naisha]], Ishikiri Kenjan Shrine, etc.<ref>[http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref> In addition, there are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, including [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], {{ill|Nikko Tōshō-gū|ja|日光東照宮}}, [[Keta Shrine]], {{ill|Nashiki-jinja|ja|梨木神社}}, [[Shin-Kumano Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Ketai-sha]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]], and others.
There are also umbrella organizations (umbrella religious corporations) other than the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "Shrine Shinto." == The term Shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to strengthen the centralized. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched.<ref>[[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (Shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref>[[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected the [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were being forced to worship at shrines ("Resolution on Shrines"). A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[Freedom of religion]] of the [[Meiji Constitution|Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but failed to pass. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "[[State Shinto]]" and "Shrine Shinto.<ref>[[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, the term Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.

{{Short description|Mainstream non-doctrinal Shinto}} {{copy edit|date=May 2022}}
'''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of [[Shinto]]. It has two main varieties, a pre-World War II variant of "[[State Shinto]]", and a form of Shinto centered on [[Shinto shrine]]s after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy and reverends.<ref>[[景山春樹]] 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref>
Today, the term Shinto usually refers to Shrine Shinto. There are many shrines throughout Japan that serve as places of worship, but until 1945 all shrines belonging to Shinto were under the jurisdiction of the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}, an external department of the [[Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945 abolished the {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}} and was reorganized as one [[Religious corporation]], establishing a new [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
There are 80,000 Shinto shrines under the jurisdiction of {{Interlanguage link|Shintoin|ja|神祇院}}, consisting of those that came under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] when it was established (approx. 7,800 shrines), those that came from a different umbrella organization of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], and ones that became {{ill|Standalone Shrine|lt=Standalone Shrines|ja|単立}}.
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as [[Kojiki]] and [[Nihon Shoki]]. The [[Kannushi]] are in charge of the rituals, and they hold positions such as priests, priests-in-charge, [[Kannushi]], and administers of the rituals. The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the Shinto priesthood. A [[Kagura]] dancer may participate in Shinto rituals, but may not preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
There are two universities and seven technical/vocational schools that offer training programs for the priesthood, and the length of study ranges from one to four years. There are no examples of shrines under the guidance of an umbrella organization other than the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] or single shrines establishing their own qualifications for priesthood or conferring their own "ranks". These educational institutions accept students from shrines not under the umbrella of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]] and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref>「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2年(2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref>[https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
== Umbrella organization == Shinto was once supposed to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]], with the Shinto Secretariat being [[Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent. However, due to the defeat of the Axis powers in [[World War II]] soon after, it did not achieve any notable results.
After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]], successor to the Jingion, was the organization to which the largest number of shrines belonged. But since techinically the [[Religious corporation]] status is a condition for membership, small shrines that do not have a juridical personality are not eligible and are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. In addition, even for shrines with a religious juridical personality, such as in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]], more than half of these shrines are not members of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].<ref>[http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>
There are several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, such as [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Nikko Toshogu]], [[Keta Shrine]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], and [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hizengu Shrine]].
There are also umbrella organizations (religious corporations) other than the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]]. 91 Shinto Shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to the Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to the Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== Origin of the term "Shrine Shinto." == The term Shrine Shinto is relatively new and was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to strengthen centralization. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was not enough to strengthen the centralized system, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign for the proclamation of the Imperial Way was launched.<ref>[[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and [[Festival|Ritual]] (Shrine Shinto) were separated, and [[Shinto]] was declared non-religious as a national ritual.<ref>[[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected the [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were being forced to worship at shrines. A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[Freedom of religion]] in the [[Meiji Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>
The Religious Organizations Act of 1899, which did not include Shintoism or Christianity, was introduced to Congress three times but never passed. Shinto shrines were also included in the Shinto religion, and although they did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism, they were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
Some influential scholars called it national Shinto and subdivided it into "[[State Shinto]]" and "Shrine Shinto".<ref>[[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, the term Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.

Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as ''[[Kojiki]]'' and [[Nihon Shoki|''Nihon Shoki'']]. The ''[[Kannushi]]'' are in charge of the rituals, holding positions such as priests, priests-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the priesthood. Outside the Association, 91 shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史}}, 23 to Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29年版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>

In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to centralize administration. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was unsuccessful, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign to proclaim the [[Imperial Way Faction|Imperial Way]] was launched.<ref>[[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and Shrine Shinto separated, and declared that [[Shinto]] was a national, not religious, ritual.<ref>[[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>

In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were forced to worship at shrines. A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[freedom of religion]] specified in the [[Meiji Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>
[[State Shinto]] and Shrine Shinto were named in 1924.<ref>[[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.

In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to centralize administration. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was unsuccessful, so the [[Ministry of Shinto]] was established and a campaign to proclaim the [[Imperial Way Faction|Imperial Way]] was launched.<ref>[[文部省]]・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『[[学制百年史]]』（1972年）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]] (denominational Shinto and Buddhism) and Shrine Shinto separated, and declared that [[Shinto]] was a national, not religious, ritual.<ref>[[武田政一]] 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
Shinto shrines were included in the Shinto religion, but did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism. They were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were forced to worship at shrines. A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[freedom of religion]] specified in the [[Meiji Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50年史』62頁。</ref>
[[State Shinto]] and Shrine Shinto were named in 1924.<ref>[[加藤玄智]]（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は[[1924年]]（大正13年）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
Shinto was once to be administered by the [[Shinto Secretariat]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]], with the Shinto Secretariat called [[Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement (Japan)|National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent.{{Citation needed|date=May 2022}}
