{{draft categories| [[Category:Shinto]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia 6]] [[Category:qq]] [[Category:11 6]] [[Category:mm]] [[Category:Pages with unreviewed translations]] [[Category:History of Shinto]] [[Category:11]] [[Category:Shrine Shinto]] [[Category:New]] [[Category:Pages and Categories from Wikipedia]] }} [[sn:Shrine Shinto]] [[en:Shrine Shinto]] [[ja:Shrine Shinto]]

{{Short description|Mainstream non-doctrinal Shinto}} '''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of the [[Shinto]] religion.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: J |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_j.html#Jinja_Shinto |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It has two main varieties: [[State Shinto]], a pre-World War II variant, and another centered on [[Shinto shrine]]s after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy.<ref>景山春樹 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref><ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
Today, the term Shinto usually refers to Shrine Shinto. Shrines throughout Japan serve as places of worship. Until 1945 all Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of the [[Institute of Divinities]], an external department of the [[Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945 abolished the [[Institute of Divinities]], which was reorganized as one [[religious corporation]], the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. The ''[[Kannushi]]'' are in charge of the rituals, holding positions such as priests, priests-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the priesthood. A ''[[Kagura]]'' dancer may participate in Shinto rituals, but may not preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
== Education == Two universities and seven technical/vocational schools exclusively offer training programs for the priesthood. The length of study ranges from one to four years. These educational institutions accept students from standalone shrines and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref>「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2nd year (2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref>[https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
These two universities are [[Kokugakuin University]], and [[Kogakkan University]].<ref>Kogakkan University Faculty of Letters: Kogakkan University official website [https://web.archive.org/web/20150316092725/http://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/english/faculty/index.php]</ref><ref>S.D.B.Picken "Faith-based schools in Japan: Paradoxes and Pointers". In J.D. Chapman et al. (eds.) "International Handbook of Learning, Teaching and Leading in Faith-Based Schools". New York: Springer. P. 523.</ref>
== Administration == After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] supervised the largest number of shrines. But since technically the religious corporation status is a condition for membership, small shrines that do not have a juridical character are not eligible and are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. Fewer than half of juridical shrines, such as in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]] are not members of the Association.<ref>[http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>
Several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, such as [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Keta Taisha|Keta Shrine]], [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], and [[Hinokuma Jingu]] are not members of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], but still considered as Shrine Shinto rather than [[Sect Shinto]].
80,000 Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of [[Institute of Divinities]], consisting of 7,800 managed by the Association of Shinto Shrines and others that became standalone shrines. Outside the Association, 91 shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|12=draft}}, 23 to Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29th year 版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== History == The term Shrine Shinto was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to centralize administration. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was unsuccessful, so the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was established and a campaign to [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine|proclaim the Great Doctrine]] began.<ref>文部省・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『学制百年史』（1972nd year ）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]]. As a result "religions" such as [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]] and Shrine Shinto separated, and declared that [[Shinto]] was a national, not religious, ritual.<ref>武田政一 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
Shinto shrines were included in the Shinto religion, but did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism. They were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were forced to worship at shrines. A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[freedom of religion]] specified in the [[Meiji Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50th year 史』62頁。</ref>
[[State Shinto]] and Shrine Shinto were named in 1924.<ref>加藤玄智（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は1924th year （大正13rd year ）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
Shinto was once to be administered by the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]], and the Bureau itself made a sect called [[Shintō Taikyō|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent.{{Citation needed|date=May 2022}}

== Education == Two universities and seven technical/vocational schools exclusively offer training programs for the priesthood. The length of study ranges from one to four years. These educational institutions accept students from standalone shrines and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref>「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2nd year (2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref>[https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
These two universities are [[Kokugakuin University]], and [[Kogakkan University]].<ref>Kogakkan University Faculty of Letters: Kogakkan University official website [https://web.archive.org/web/20150316092725/http://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/english/faculty/index.php]</ref><ref>S.D.B.Picken "Faith-based schools in Japan: Paradoxes and Pointers". In J.D. Chapman et al. (eds.) "International Handbook of Learning, Teaching and Leading in Faith-Based Schools". New York: Springer. P. 523.</ref>
== Administration == After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] supervised the largest number of shrines. But since technically the religious corporation status is a condition for membership, small shrines that do not have a juridical character are not eligible and are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. Fewer than half of juridical shrines, such as in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]] are not members of the Association.<ref>[http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>
Several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, such as [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Keta Taisha|Keta Shrine]], [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], and [[Hinokuma Jingu]] are not members of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], but still considered as Shrine Shinto rather than [[Sect Shinto]].
80,000 Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of [[Institute of Divinities]], consisting of 7,800 managed by the Association of Shinto Shrines and others that became standalone shrines. Outside the Association, 91 shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|12=draft}}, 23 to Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29th year 版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== History == The term Shrine Shinto was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to centralize administration. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was unsuccessful, so the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was established and a campaign to [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine|proclaim the Great Doctrine]] began.<ref>文部省・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『学制百年史』（1972nd year ）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]]. As a result "religions" such as [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]] and Shrine Shinto separated, and declared that [[Shinto]] was a national, not religious, ritual.<ref>武田政一 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
Shinto shrines were included in the Shinto religion, but did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism. They were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were forced to worship at shrines. A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[freedom of religion]] specified in the [[Meiji Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50th year 史』62頁。</ref>
[[State Shinto]] and Shrine Shinto were named in 1924.<ref>加藤玄智（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は1924th year （大正13rd year ）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
Shinto was once to be administered by the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]], and the Bureau itself made a sect called [[Shintō Taikyō|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent.{{Citation needed|date=May 2022}}
== See also == *[[Seicho-no-Ie]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Secular Shrine Theory]] *[[Shinto sects and schools]] *[[Sect Shinto]]

]]

{{Short description|Mainstream non-doctrinal Shinto}} '''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of the [[Shinto]] religion.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: J |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_j.html#Jinja_Shinto |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It has two main varieties: [[State Shinto]], a pre-World War II variant, and another centered on [[Shinto shrine]]s after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy.<ref>景山春樹 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref><ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
Today, the term Shinto usually refers to Shrine Shinto. Shrines throughout Japan serve as places of worship. Until 1945 all Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of the [[Institute of Divinities]], an external department of the [[Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945 abolished the [[Institute of Divinities]], which was reorganized as one [[religious corporation]], the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. The ''[[Kannushi]]'' are in charge of the rituals, holding positions such as priests, priests-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the priesthood. A ''[[Kagura]]'' dancer may participate in Shinto rituals, but may not preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
== Education == Two universities and seven technical/vocational schools exclusively offer training programs for the priesthood. The length of study ranges from one to four years. These educational institutions accept students from standalone shrines and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref>「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2nd year (2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref>[https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
These two universities are [[Kokugakuin University]], and [[Kogakkan University]].<ref>Kogakkan University Faculty of Letters: Kogakkan University official website [https://web.archive.org/web/20150316092725/http://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/english/faculty/index.php]</ref><ref>S.D.B.Picken "Faith-based schools in Japan: Paradoxes and Pointers". In J.D. Chapman et al. (eds.) "International Handbook of Learning, Teaching and Leading in Faith-Based Schools". New York: Springer. P. 523.</ref>
== Administration == After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] supervised the largest number of shrines. But since technically the religious corporation status is a condition for membership, small shrines that do not have a juridical character are not eligible and are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. Several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, such as [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Keta Taisha|Keta Shrine]], [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], and [[Hinokuma Jingu]] are not members of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], but still considered as Shrine Shinto rather than [[Sect Shinto]].
80,000 Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of [[Institute of Divinities]], consisting of 7,800 managed by the Association of Shinto Shrines and others that became standalone shrines. Outside the Association, 91 shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|12=draft}}, 23 to Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29th year 版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== History == The term Shrine Shinto was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to centralize administration. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was unsuccessful, so the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was established and a campaign to [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine|proclaim the Great Doctrine]] began.<ref>文部省・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『学制百年史』（1972nd year ）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]]. As a result "religions" such as [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]] and Shrine Shinto separated, and declared that [[Shinto]] was a national, not religious, ritual.<ref>武田政一 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>

The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent.{{Citation needed|date=May 2022}}

== Education == Two universities and seven technical/vocational schools exclusively offer training programs for the priesthood. The length of study ranges from one to four years. These educational institutions accept students from standalone shrines and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref>「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2nd year (2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref>[https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
These two universities are [[Kokugakuin University]], and [[Kogakkan University]].<ref>Kogakkan University Faculty of Letters: Kogakkan University official website [https://web.archive.org/web/20150316092725/http://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/english/faculty/index.php]</ref><ref>S.D.B.Picken "Faith-based schools in Japan: Paradoxes and Pointers". In J.D. Chapman et al. (eds.) "International Handbook of Learning, Teaching and Leading in Faith-Based Schools". New York: Springer. P. 523.</ref>
== Administration == After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] supervised the largest number of shrines. But since technically the religious corporation status is a condition for membership, small shrines that do not have a juridical character are not eligible and are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. Fewer than half of juridical shrines, such as in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]] are not members of the Association.<ref>[http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>
Several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, such as [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Keta Taisha|Keta Shrine]], [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], and [[Hinokuma Jingu]] are not members of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], but still considered as Shrine Shinto rather than [[Sect Shinto]].
80,000 Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of [[Institute of Divinities]], consisting of 7,800 managed by the Association of Shinto Shrines and others that became standalone shrines. Outside the Association, 91 shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 23 to Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29th year 版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== History == The term Shrine Shinto was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to centralize administration. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was unsuccessful, so the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was established and a campaign to [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine|proclaim the Great Doctrine]] began.<ref>文部省・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『学制百年史』（1972nd year ）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]]. As a result "religions" such as [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]] and Shrine Shinto separated, and declared that [[Shinto]] was a national, not religious, ritual.<ref>武田政一 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
Shinto shrines were included in the Shinto religion, but did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism. They were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were forced to worship at shrines. A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[freedom of religion]] specified in the [[Meiji Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50th year 史』62頁。</ref>
[[State Shinto]] and Shrine Shinto were named in 1924.<ref>加藤玄智（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は1924th year （大正13rd year ）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
Shinto was once to be administered by the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]], and the Bureau itself made a sect called [[Shintō Taikyō|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent.{{Citation needed|date=May 2022}}

{{Short description|Mainstream non-doctrinal Shinto}} '''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of the [[Shinto]] religion.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: J |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_j.html#Jinja_Shinto |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It has two main varieties: [[State Shinto]], a pre-World War II variant, and another centered on [[Shinto shrine]]s after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy.<ref>景山春樹 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref><ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
Today, the term Shinto usually refers to Shrine Shinto. Shrines throughout Japan serve as places of worship. Until 1945 all Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of the [[Institute of Divinities]], an external department of the [[Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945 abolished the [[Institute of Divinities]], which was reorganized as one [[religious corporation]], the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. The ''[[Kannushi]]'' are in charge of the rituals, holding positions such as priests, priests-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the priesthood. A ''[[Kagura]]'' dancer may participate in Shinto rituals, but may not preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
== Education == Two universities and seven technical/vocational schools exclusively offer training programs for the priesthood. The length of study ranges from one to four years. These educational institutions accept students from standalone shrines and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref>「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2nd year (2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref>[https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
These two universities are [[Kokugakuin University]], and [[Kogakkan University]].<ref>Kogakkan University Faculty of Letters: Kogakkan University official website [https://web.archive.org/web/20150316092725/http://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/english/faculty/index.php]</ref><ref>S.D.B.Picken "Faith-based schools in Japan: Paradoxes and Pointers". In J.D. Chapman et al. (eds.) "International Handbook of Learning, Teaching and Leading in Faith-Based Schools". New York: Springer. P. 523.</ref>

But since technically the religious corporation status is a condition for membership, small shrines that do not have a juridical character are not eligible and are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. Fewer than half of juridical shrines, such as in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]] are not members of the Association.<ref>[http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>
Several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, such as [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Keta Taisha|Keta Shrine]], [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], and [[Hinokuma Jingu]] are not members of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], but still considered as Shrine Shinto rather than [[Sect Shinto]].
80,000 Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of [[Institute of Divinities]], consisting of 7,800 managed by the Association of Shinto Shrines and others that became standalone shrines. Outside the Association, 91 shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 23 to Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29th year 版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>

On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]]. == Administration == After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] supervised the largest number of shrines. But since technically the religious corporation status is a condition for membership, small shrines that do not have a juridical character are not eligible and are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. Fewer than half of juridical shrines, such as in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]] are not members of the Association.<ref>[http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>
Several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, such as [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Keta Taisha|Keta Shrine]], [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], and [[Hinokuma Jingu]] are not members of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], but still considered as Shrine Shinto rather than [[Sect Shinto]].
80,000 Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of [[Institute of Divinities]], consisting of 7,800 managed by the Association of Shinto Shrines and others that became standalone shrines. Outside the Association, 91 shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=draft|qq=draft}}, 23 to Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29th year 版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== History == The term Shrine Shinto was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to centralize administration. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was unsuccessful, so the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was established and a campaign to [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine|proclaim the Great Doctrine]] began.<ref>文部省・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『学制百年史』（1972nd year ）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]]. As a result "religions" such as [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]] and Shrine Shinto separated, and declared that [[Shinto]] was a national, not religious, ritual.<ref>武田政一 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
Shinto shrines were included in the Shinto religion, but did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism. They were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were forced to worship at shrines. A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[freedom of religion]] specified in the [[Meiji Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50th year 史』62頁。</ref>
[[State Shinto]] and Shrine Shinto were named in 1924.<ref>加藤玄智（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は1924th year （大正13rd year ）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
Shinto was once to be administered by the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]], and the Bureau itself made a sect called [[Shintō Taikyō|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent.{{Citation needed|date=May 2022}}
== See also == *[[Seicho-no-Ie]] *[[List of Shinto shrines]] *[[Secular Shrine Theory]] *[[Shinto sects and schools]] *[[Sect Shinto]]

{{Short description|Mainstream non-doctrinal Shinto}} '''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of the [[Shinto]] religion.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: J |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_j.html#Jinja_Shinto |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It has two main varieties: [[State Shinto]], a pre-World War II variant, and another centered on [[Shinto shrine]]s after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy.<ref>景山春樹 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref><ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
Today, the term Shinto usually refers to Shrine Shinto. Shrines throughout Japan serve as places of worship. Until 1945 all Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of the [[Institute of Divinities]], an external department of the [[Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945 abolished the [[Institute of Divinities]], which was reorganized as one [[religious corporation]], the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. The ''[[Kannushi]]'' are in charge of the rituals, holding positions such as priests, priests-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the priesthood. A ''[[Kagura]]'' dancer may participate in Shinto rituals, but may not preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
== Education == Two universities and seven technical/vocational schools exclusively offer training programs for the priesthood. The length of study ranges from one to four years. These educational institutions accept students from standalone shrines and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref>「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2nd year (2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref>[https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
These two universities are [[Kokugakuin University]], and [[Kogakkan University]].<ref>Kogakkan University Faculty of Letters: Kogakkan University official website [https://web.archive.org/web/20150316092725/http://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/english/faculty/index.php]</ref><ref>S.D.B.Picken "Faith-based schools in Japan: Paradoxes and Pointers". In J.D. Chapman et al. (eds.) "International Handbook of Learning, Teaching and Leading in Faith-Based Schools". New York: Springer. P. 523.</ref>
== Administration == After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] supervised the largest number of shrines. But since technically the religious corporation status is a condition for membership, small shrines that do not have a juridical character are not eligible and are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. Fewer than half of juridical shrines, such as in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]] are not members of the Association.<ref>[http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>
Several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, such as [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Keta Taisha|Keta Shrine]], [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], and [[Hinokuma Jingu]] are not members of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], but still considered as Shrine Shinto rather than [[Sect Shinto]].
80,000 Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of [[Institute of Divinities]], consisting of 7,800 managed by the Association of Shinto Shrines and others that became standalone shrines. Outside the Association, 91 shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=}}, 23 to Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29th year 版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== History == The term Shrine Shinto was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to centralize administration. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was unsuccessful, so the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was established and a campaign to [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine|proclaim the Great Doctrine]] began.<ref>文部省・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『学制百年史』（1972nd year ）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]]. As a result "religions" such as [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]] and Shrine Shinto separated, and declared that [[Shinto]] was a national, not religious, ritual.<ref>武田政一 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>

Shinto was once to be administered by the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]], and the Bureau itself made a sect called [[Shintō Taikyō|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent.{{Citation needed|date=May 2022}}

Several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, such as [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Keta Taisha|Keta Shrine]], [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], and [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hinokuma Jingu]] are not members of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], but still considered as Shrine Shinto rather than [[Sect Shinto]].
80,000 Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of [[Institute of Divinities]], consisting of 7,800 managed by the Association of Shinto Shrines and others that became standalone shrines. Outside the Association, 91 shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=}}, 23 to Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29th year 版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== History == The term Shrine Shinto was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to centralize administration. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was unsuccessful, so the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was established and a campaign to [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine|proclaim the Great Doctrine]] began.<ref>文部省・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『学制百年史』（1972nd year ）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]]. As a result "religions" such as [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]] and Shrine Shinto separated, and declared that [[Shinto]] was a national, not religious, ritual.<ref>武田政一 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
Shinto shrines were included in the Shinto religion, but did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism. They were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were forced to worship at shrines. A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[freedom of religion]] specified in the [[Meiji Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50th year 史』62頁。</ref>
[[State Shinto]] and Shrine Shinto were named in 1924.<ref>加藤玄智（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は1924th year （大正13rd year ）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
Shinto was once to be administered by the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]], and the Bureau itself made a sect called [[Shintō Taikyō|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent.{{Citation needed|date=May 2022}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Mainstream non-doctrinal Shinto}} '''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of the [[Shinto]] religion.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: J |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_j.html#Jinja_Shinto |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It has two main varieties: [[State Shinto]], a pre-World War II variant, and another centered on [[Shinto shrine]]s after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy.<ref>景山春樹 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref><ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
Today, the term Shinto usually refers to Shrine Shinto. Shrines throughout Japan serve as places of worship. Until 1945 all Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of the [[Institute of Divinities]], an external department of the [[Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945 abolished the [[Institute of Divinities]], which was reorganized as one [[religious corporation]], the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. The ''[[Kannushi]]'' are in charge of the rituals, holding positions such as priests, priests-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the priesthood. A ''[[Kagura]]'' dancer may participate in Shinto rituals, but may not preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
== Education == Two universities and seven technical/vocational schools exclusively offer training programs for the priesthood. The length of study ranges from one to four years. These educational institutions accept students from standalone shrines and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref>「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2nd year (2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref>[https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
These two universities are [[Kokugakuin University]], and [[Kogakkan University]].<ref>Kogakkan University Faculty of Letters: Kogakkan University official website [https://web.archive.org/web/20150316092725/http://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/english/faculty/index.php]</ref><ref>S.D.B.Picken "Faith-based schools in Japan: Paradoxes and Pointers". In J.D. Chapman et al. (eds.) "International Handbook of Learning, Teaching and Leading in Faith-Based Schools". New York: Springer. P. 523.</ref>
== Administration == After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] supervised the largest number of shrines. But since technically the religious corporation status is a condition for membership, small shrines that do not have a juridical character are not eligible and are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. Fewer than half of juridical shrines, such as in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]] are not members of the Association.<ref>[http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>
Several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, such as [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Keta Taisha|Keta Shrine]], [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], and [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hinokuma Jingu]] are not members of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], but still considered as Shrine Shinto rather than [[Sect Shinto]].
80,000 Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of [[Institute of Divinities]], consisting of 7,800 managed by the Association of Shinto Shrines and others that became standalone shrines. Outside the Association, 91 shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=}}, 23 to Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29th year 版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== History == The term Shrine Shinto was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to centralize administration. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was unsuccessful, so the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was established and a campaign to [[Proclamation of the Great Doctrine|proclaim the Great Doctrine]] began.<ref>文部省・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『学制百年史』（1972nd year ）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]]. As a result "religions" such as [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]] and Shrine Shinto separated, and declared that [[Shinto]] was a national, not religious, ritual.<ref>武田政一 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
Shinto shrines were included in the Shinto religion, but did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism. They were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were forced to worship at shrines. A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[freedom of religion]] specified in the [[Meiji Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50th year 史』62頁。</ref>
[[State Shinto]] and Shrine Shinto were named in 1924.<ref>加藤玄智（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は1924th year （大正13rd year ）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
Shinto was once to be administered by the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]], and the Bureau itself made a sect called [[Shintō Taikyō|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent.{{Citation needed|date=May 2022}}

80,000 Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of [[Institute of Divinities]], consisting of 7,800 managed by the Association of Shinto Shrines and others that became standalone shrines. Outside the Association, 91 shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=}}, 23 to Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29th year 版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== History == The term Shrine Shinto was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to centralize administration. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was unsuccessful, so the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was established and a campaign to [[Taikyo Proclamation|proclaim the Great Doctrine]] began.<ref>文部省・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『学制百年史』（1972nd year ）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]]. As a result "religions" such as [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]] and Shrine Shinto separated, and declared that [[Shinto]] was a national, not religious, ritual.<ref>武田政一 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
Shinto shrines were included in the Shinto religion, but did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism. They were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were forced to worship at shrines. A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[freedom of religion]] specified in the [[Meiji Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50th year 史』62頁。</ref>
[[State Shinto]] and Shrine Shinto were named in 1924.<ref>加藤玄智（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は1924th year （大正13rd year ）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
Shinto was once to be administered by the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]], and the Bureau itself made a sect called [[Shintō Taikyō|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent.{{Citation needed|date=May 2022}}

{{Short description|Mainstream non-doctrinal Shinto}} '''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of the [[Shinto]] religion.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: J |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_j.html#Jinja_Shinto |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It has two main varieties: [[State Shinto]], a pre-World War II variant, and another centered on [[Shinto shrine]]s after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy.<ref>景山春樹 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref><ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
Today, the term Shinto usually refers to Shrine Shinto. Shrines throughout Japan serve as places of worship. Until 1945 all Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of the [[Institute of Divinities]], an external department of the [[Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945 abolished the [[Institute of Divinities]], which was reorganized as one [[religious corporation]], the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. The ''[[Kannushi]]'' are in charge of the rituals, holding positions such as priests, priests-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the priesthood. A ''[[Kagura]]'' dancer may participate in Shinto rituals, but may not preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
== Education == Two universities and seven technical/vocational schools exclusively offer training programs for the priesthood. The length of study ranges from one to four years. These educational institutions accept students from standalone shrines and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref>「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2nd year (2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref>[https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
These two universities are [[Kokugakuin University]], and [[Kogakkan University]].<ref>Kogakkan University Faculty of Letters: Kogakkan University official website [https://web.archive.org/web/20150316092725/http://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/english/faculty/index.php]</ref><ref>S.D.B.Picken "Faith-based schools in Japan: Paradoxes and Pointers". In J.D. Chapman et al. (eds.) "International Handbook of Learning, Teaching and Leading in Faith-Based Schools". New York: Springer. P. 523.</ref>
== Administration == After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] supervised the largest number of shrines. But since technically the religious corporation status is a condition for membership, small shrines that do not have a juridical character are not eligible and are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. Fewer than half of juridical shrines, such as in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]] are not members of the Association.<ref>[http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>
Several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, such as [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Keta Taisha|Keta Shrine]], [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], and [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hinokuma Jingu]] are not members of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], but still considered as Shrine Shinto rather than [[Sect Shinto]].
80,000 Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of [[Institute of Divinities]], consisting of 7,800 managed by the Association of Shinto Shrines and others that became standalone shrines. Outside the Association, 91 shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=}}, 23 to Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29th year 版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== History == The term Shrine Shinto was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to centralize administration. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was unsuccessful, so the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was established and a campaign to [[Taikyo Proclamation|proclaim the Great Doctrine]] began.<ref>文部省・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『学制百年史』（1972nd year ）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]]. As a result "religions" such as [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]] and Shrine Shinto separated, and declared that [[Shinto]] was a national, not religious, ritual.<ref>武田政一 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
Shinto shrines were included in the Shinto religion, but did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism. They were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were forced to worship at shrines. A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[freedom of religion]] specified in the [[Meiji Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50th year 史』62頁。</ref>
[[State Shinto]] and Shrine Shinto were named in 1924.<ref>加藤玄智（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は1924th year （大正13rd year ）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
Shinto was once to be administered by the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]], and the Bureau itself made a sect called [[Shintō Taikyō|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent.{{Citation needed|date=May 2022}}

80,000 Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of [[Institute of Divinities]], consisting of 7,800 managed by the Association of Shinto Shrines and others that became standalone shrines. Outside the Association, 91 shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=}}, 23 to Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29th year 版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== History == The term Shrine Shinto was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to centralize administration. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was unsuccessful, so the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was established and a campaign to [[Taikyo Proclamation|proclaim the Great Doctrine]] began.<ref>文部省・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『学制百年史』（1972nd year ）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]]. As a result "religions" such as [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] and [[Buddhism]] and Shrine Shinto separated, and declared that [[Shinto]] was a national, not religious, ritual.<ref>武田政一 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
Shinto shrines were included in the Shinto religion, but did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism. They were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were forced to worship at shrines. A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[:en:Freedom of religion|Freedom of religion]] specified in the [[Meiji Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50th year 史』62頁。</ref>
[[State Shinto]] and Shrine Shinto were named in 1924.<ref>加藤玄智（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は1924th year （大正13rd year ）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
Shinto was once to be administered by the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]], and the Bureau itself made a sect called [[Shintō Taikyō|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent.{{Citation needed|date=May 2022}}

revision.text:
{{Short description|Mainstream non-doctrinal Shinto}} '''Shrine Shinto''' is a form of the [[Shinto]] religion.<ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a">{{Cite web |title=Basic Terms of Shinto: J |url=https://www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp/ijcc/wp/bts/bts_j.html#Jinja_Shinto |access-date=2023-03-10 |website=www2.kokugakuin.ac.jp}}</ref> It has two main varieties: [[State Shinto]], a pre-World War II variant, and another centered on [[Shinto shrine]]s after World War II, in which ritual rites are the center of belief, conducted by an organization of clergy.<ref>景山春樹 「神道」『世界大百科事典』 219頁。</ref><ref name="www2kokugakuinacjp-2023a" />
Today, the term Shinto usually refers to Shrine Shinto. Shrines throughout Japan serve as places of worship. Until 1945 all Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of the [[Institute of Divinities]], an external department of the [[Home Ministry]]. The [[Shinto Directive]] issued by the [[Supreme Commander for the Allied Powers|GHQ]] in December 1945 abolished the [[Institute of Divinities]], which was reorganized as one [[religious corporation]], the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]].
Shinto shrines and Shinto rituals are performed in accordance with sacred texts such as ''[[Kojiki]]'' and ''[[Nihon Shoki]]''. The ''[[Kannushi]]'' are in charge of the rituals, holding positions such as priests, priests-in-charge, and administer the rituals. The [[miko]] of Shinto shrines are not included in the priesthood. A ''[[Kagura]]'' dancer may participate in Shinto rituals, but may not preside over or perform Shinto rituals.
== Education == Two universities and seven technical/vocational schools exclusively offer training programs for the priesthood. The length of study ranges from one to four years. These educational institutions accept students from standalone shrines and from shrines belonging to [[Sect Shinto]].<ref>「[http://img.kokugakuin.ac.jp/assets/uploads/2019/06/0579697b998699d131c2305c7879c53e.pdf 宗教特別専攻]」,『国学院大学 令和2nd year (2020)入学試験要項』2019,p.8(2021.8.30閲覧)。</ref><ref>[https://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/campusview/admissions/adm_06.php 神職後継者専攻:神社系教団用],『皇學館大学受験生サイトCampus Vew』,出願書類のダウンロード>AO入試神職後継専攻のみ(2021.8.30閲覧).</ref>
These two universities are [[Kokugakuin University]], and [[Kogakkan University]].<ref>Kogakkan University Faculty of Letters: Kogakkan University official website [https://web.archive.org/web/20150316092725/http://www.kogakkan-u.ac.jp/english/faculty/index.php]</ref><ref>S.D.B.Picken "Faith-based schools in Japan: Paradoxes and Pointers". In J.D. Chapman et al. (eds.) "International Handbook of Learning, Teaching and Leading in Faith-Based Schools". New York: Springer. P. 523.</ref>
== Administration == After World War II, the [[Association of Shinto Shrines|Jinja Honcho]] supervised the largest number of shrines. But since technically the religious corporation status is a condition for membership, small shrines that do not have a juridical character are not eligible and are not allowed to belong to the Honcho. Fewer than half of juridical shrines, such as in [[Higashiosaka|Higashiosaka City]] are not members of the Association.<ref>[http://www.jinjacho-osaka.net/osakafunai-no-jinjya/dai6sibu/higasiosaka-city/m28.html 大阪府神社庁 第六支部 東大阪市]</ref>
Several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, such as [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Keta Taisha|Keta Shrine]], [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], and [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hinokuma Jingu]] are not members of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], but still considered as Shrine Shinto rather than [[Sect Shinto]].
80,000 Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of [[Institute of Divinities]], consisting of 7,800 managed by the Association of Shinto Shrines and others that became standalone shrines. Outside the Association, 91 shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=}}, 23 to Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29th year 版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== History == The term Shrine Shinto was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to centralize administration. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was unsuccessful, so the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was established and a campaign to [[Taikyo Proclamation|proclaim the Great Doctrine]] began.<ref>文部省・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『学制百年史』（1972nd year ）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]]. As a result "religions" such as [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] and [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] and Shrine Shinto separated, and declared that [[Shinto]] was a national, not religious, ritual.<ref>武田政一 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
Shinto shrines were included in the Shinto religion, but did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism. They were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were forced to worship at shrines. A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[:en:Freedom of religion|Freedom of religion]] specified in the [[Meiji Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50th year 史』62頁。</ref>
[[State Shinto]] and Shrine Shinto were named in 1924.<ref>加藤玄智（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は1924th year （大正13rd year ）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
Shinto was once to be administered by the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]], and the Bureau itself made a sect called [[Shintō Taikyō|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent.{{Citation needed|date=May 2022}}

Several Shinto shrines throughout Japan, such as [[Kamakura-gū]], [[Yasukuni Shrine]], [[Fushimi Inari-taisha]], [[Keta Taisha|Keta Shrine]], [[Nikkō Tōshō-gū|Nikko Toshogu]], [[Tomioka Hachiman Shrine]], and [[Hinokuma Shrine|Hinokuma Jingu]] are not members of the [[Association of Shinto Shrines]], but still considered as Shrine Shinto rather than [[Sect Shinto]].
80,000 Shinto shrines were under the jurisdiction of [[Institute of Divinities]], consisting of 7,800 managed by the Association of Shinto Shrines and others that became standalone shrines. Outside the Association, 91 shrines belong to Seishin Meiseikai, 78 to Jinja Honkyo, 72 to Jinja Sandonkyo, 60 to {{ill|History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|lt=Hokkaido Shrine Association|ja|北海道の神社の歴史|1=History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=draft|qq=draft|12=simple|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|13=User:Immanuelle/History of Shinto shrines in Hokkaido|qq=}}, 23 to Japan Jingu Honcho, and 15 to Japan Shrine Kyodan.<ref>『平成29th year 版 宗教年鑑』参照</ref>
== History == The term Shrine Shinto was created to distinguish it from [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] after the [[Meiji (era)|Meiji]] era. In 1868, the [[Meiji Restoration|Restoration Government]] established the [[Department of Divinities]] to centralize administration. However, the [[State Shinto]] policy was unsuccessful, so the [[Ministry of Divinities]] was established and a campaign to [[Taikyo Proclamation|proclaim the Great Doctrine]] began.<ref>文部省・学制百年史編集委員会「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120820061534/https://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317735.htm 明治初期における宗教行政]」 、『学制百年史』（1972nd year ）「[https://web.archive.org/web/20120420152929/http://www.mext.go.jp/b_menu/hakusho/html/others/detail/1317885.htm 編集後記]」。</ref>
On January 24, 1882, the Ministry of the Interior issued Ministerial Instruction No. B7, "Jikko-kamikami ha kyōdōshi ni kyōdōshi ni kyōsoku wo kyōsoku wo kyōdōshi no kanryūmono mono to sasaru mono" ("Shinto Priests are not a religion") and declared that [[Secular Shrine Theory]]. As a result "religions" such as [[Sect Shinto|denominational Shinto]] and [[:en:Buddhism|Buddhism]] and Shrine Shinto separated, and declared that [[Shinto]] was a national, not religious, ritual.<ref>武田政一 「神社」『世界大百科事典』 118頁。</ref>
Although the Religion Bill of 1899 did not include [[Shinto]] or [[:en:Christianity|Christianity]], denominational Shinto began to be referred to as national Shinto toward the end of the Meiji period.
Shinto shrines were included in the Shinto religion, but did not have the same teachings and precepts as Buddhism. They were protected by the government until the end of World War II.
In 1917, the Japanese Christian Church rejected [[Secular Shrine Theory]] on the grounds that students were forced to worship at shrines. A further complaint was filed that the coercion violated the [[:en:Freedom of religion|Freedom of religion]] specified in the [[Meiji Constitution]].<ref>『日本キリスト教会50th year 史』62頁。</ref>
[[State Shinto]] and Shrine Shinto were named in 1924.<ref>加藤玄智（陸軍士官学校教授・東京帝国大学神道講座助教授）は1924th year （大正13rd year ）の著書『東西思想比較研究』以降、この説を展開した。</ref> Before World War II, Shrine Shinto referred to the rituals, ideology, and organization of shrines, which in modern times have been subject to government control.
Shinto was once to be administered by the [[Bureau of Shinto Affairs]], but was eventually separated from Shrine Shinto as [[Sect Shinto]], and the Bureau itself made a sect called [[Shintō Taikyō|Shinto Taikyo]]. The Shinto shrines were then administered by the [[Home Ministry]]. Under the influence of the [[National Spiritual Mobilization Movement]], they were separated from Shinto shrines in 1940, becoming independent.{{Citation needed|date=May 2022}}
